lists.label: Accessibility Links and Resources

pedagogy.philosophy: A senior practitioner learns fastest when new knowledge is recruited to solve a problem they can already feel. Each session opens with a realistic nonprofit consulting scenario — a board objection, an audit finding, an RFP question — that creates an immediate knowledge gap, then surfaces the curated resources as the evidence kit needed to close that gap. This inverts the traditional 'read then apply' pattern: the learner encounters the situation before the theory, which activates prior UX expertise as a scaffold rather than a liability. Retention mechanisms are built around consultant outputs — a response to a client email, a sentence for a deliverable, a recommendation in an audit — because producing an artifact forces retrieval and reorganization of information rather than passive recognition. Social currency is treated as a first-class learning outcome: each session explicitly flags which claims, citations, and vocabulary signal credibility in the a11y practitioner community, and which vendor or overlay narratives to treat with skepticism.

appStructure.primaryOrganization: Eight consulting clinics mapped one-to-one onto curriculum modules, each structured as a three-act scenario: (1) a triggering client moment that surfaces the knowledge gap, (2) a guided evidence kit of curated resources with annotated why-this-why-now rationale, and (3) a consultant output challenge where the learner drafts a real artifact — a reply, a recommendation, a checklist annotation — that is saved to a personal Consulting Toolkit for later reuse
appStructure.sessionUnit: One consulting clinic, estimated 30–60 minutes, comprising: one scenario setup screen, two to six resource cards with progressive-disclosure depth controls, one or two active retrieval prompts, one consultant output challenge, and a session debrief that surfaces cross-cutting themes and notable gaps relevant to that clinic
retentionMechanisms: Scenario-based activation: each clinic opens with a realistic client situation (board objection, RFP requirement, audit finding) before any resource is shown, creating a felt knowledge gap that motivates reading

Progressive disclosure resource cards: each resource card shows only the label and why-this-why-now hook by default; key topics and description expand on demand, reducing cognitive load while preserving depth for motivated exploration

Consultant output challenges: each clinic ends with a constrained drafting task — write one paragraph for a client email, annotate one checklist item, draft one procurement requirement — requiring active retrieval rather than passive review

Personal Consulting Toolkit: all drafted outputs, bookmarked resource cards, and session notes are saved to a persistent artifact library the learner can export and use in real client work, creating extrinsic motivation to complete sessions

Spaced review prompts: the app surfaces a previously completed clinic's scenario and asks the learner to recall their drafted output and the key resource they cited, shown on a distributed schedule aligned with Ebbinghaus spacing

Credibility flags: a 'social currency' annotation layer highlights which citations carry weight in the a11y community (WebAIM Million, Feingold's Structured Negotiation, Roselli on overlays) and which claims require skepticism, building the practitioner judgment that differentiates expert consultants
Freshness warnings: resources with freshnessFlag true are surfaced with a visible caution indicator and the freshnessNote, teaching the learner to evaluate source currency as a professional habit

Notable gaps sidebar: each clinic surfaces the relevant notable gaps from the curriculum meta-analysis as a 'blind spot radar,' prompting the learner to note where their current evidence kit is incomplete before advising a real client

Cross-cutting theme threading: a persistent sidebar or bottom-drawer shows which cross-cutting themes are active in the current clinic, allowing the learner to build a mental map of how modules relate rather than treating them as isolated units
appStructure.navigationModel: Default path is linear clinic-by-clinic to preserve the conceptual scaffolding from foundations to certification, but the learner can enter any clinic after completing module one; within a clinic, the three-act structure is gated so the evidence kit does not appear until the scenario has been read, and the consultant output challenge does not unlock until at least two resource cards have been opened; a dashboard view shows all eight clinics as cards with completion state, last-visited timestamp, and a count of toolkit artifacts saved from each session
progressIndicators: Clinic completion rings: a segmented ring per clinic card on the dashboard fills as the learner completes the scenario screen, opens resource cards, and submits a consultant output challenge
Toolkit artifact count: a persistent header counter showing how many reusable artifacts the learner has saved, framing progress in terms of consulting readiness rather than content consumed

Credibility score (self-assessed): after each clinic, the learner rates their confidence answering the triggering scenario on a three-point scale; the dashboard surfaces clinics where confidence remains low as priority review targets
Spaced review queue depth: a visible count of clinics due for spaced review, surfaced on the dashboard and as an optional session-start prompt, making the review schedule legible rather than opaque

Notable gaps tracker: a checklist of the eight curriculum gaps that the learner can mark as 'on my radar,' 'researching,' or 'covered,' giving a visible map of consulting readiness beyond the curated content

Cross-cutting theme completion map: a small visual matrix showing which of the eight cross-cutting themes have appeared in completed clinics, filling in as sessions are finished to make conceptual coverage visible

designDirectives: Open every clinic with a single, specific client scenario presented as a first-person consulting moment — a quoted email from a nonprofit ED, an audit finding excerpt, a board member's objection — before any resource or module framing is shown; the scenario must be written at the specificity level of real client work, not a generic 'imagine you are advising a client' prompt

Design resource cards as two-state components: collapsed state shows only the resource label and the why-this-why-now text (which is the pedagogically active hook); expanded state adds key topics as tags, a one-sentence description, and a direct link; never show the full description in the collapsed state because it reduces the motivation to read the actual resource

Treat the why-this-why-now field as the primary pedagogical text, not the description field; typographically, the why-this-why-now should be the most visually prominent text on the card after the label, set in a reading-weight typeface at comfortable size, not truncated

Gate the consultant output challenge behind a soft interaction requirement: the challenge textarea is visible but inactive with a clear message like 'Open at least two resources to unlock this challenge'; this is a motivation nudge, not a hard block — a learner who wants to skip should be able to override it with one additional click

Design the Consulting Toolkit as a genuine artifact, not a badge system: each saved output should be editable after saving, tagged with the clinic it came from, and exportable as a plain-text or markdown file; the toolkit is the learner's real deliverable from the course and should feel like a professional document, not a game reward

Surface freshness warnings (freshnessFlag true) as a distinct visual treatment on the resource card — a visible caution chip with the freshnessNote accessible on hover or tap — and include a brief explanatory tooltip the first time a learner encounters one explaining why source currency matters in standards-based consulting work

Implement the spaced review system as a lightweight prompt, not a mandatory interruption: on the dashboard, show a 'Review due' chip on clinic cards with scheduled review items; at session start, offer a one-screen recall prompt ('What was the key citation you used in your response to this scenario?') before showing the full clinic; never block the learner from accessing new content to complete a review

Design the notable gaps sidebar as a persistent but collapsible panel within each clinic, not a separate section the learner has to navigate to; each gap entry should include a one-sentence description of why the gap matters for nonprofit consulting work specifically, drawn from the curriculum's notableGaps field

Use the audienceLevel field on resource cards as a wayfinding signal, not a gate: display a small 'Foundational / Practitioner / Advanced' chip on each card so the learner can calibrate how much prior knowledge a resource assumes, but never hide or lock a resource based on level

Design the cross-cutting themes as a semantic layer the learner can toggle on: in the default view, themes are not visible; when toggled, each resource card gains a color-coded tag showing which cross-cutting themes it addresses; this allows the senior learner who prefers systems-level pattern recognition to see the connective tissue across modules without cluttering the default reading experience

Write all scenario and challenge copy in second-person present tense ('Your client's board chair has just asked...') to activate the learner's professional identity and simulate the felt stakes of real consulting moments; avoid the distancing effect of third-person case study framing

Ensure the dashboard clinic cards communicate consulting readiness, not learning progress: label completion states with practitioner-language like 'Ready to deploy,' 'In progress,' and 'Not started' rather than percentage bars or generic completion checkmarks

For the module-8 certification clinic, design the consultant output challenge as a self-assessment rubric rather than a drafting task — the learner evaluates their current knowledge against CPACC and WAS competency domains and identifies the three highest-priority gaps to address before sitting for certification; this honors the senior learner's self-directedness while providing structure

Implement responsive layout that treats the clinic as the primary unit on all screen sizes: on mobile, the three-act structure becomes a vertical sequence with full-width cards and a sticky bottom bar showing clinic progress and the toolkit save action; do not attempt to show the full dashboard and clinic content simultaneously on small screens

Apply a typographic and color system that reads as professional-grade consulting material, not an educational platform: use a restrained palette, editorial typography, and generous whitespace; the visual language should feel closer to a premium research brief than a learning management system, because the learner's identity is consultant, not student

modules.moduleTitle: Foundations: why accessibility matters and who it serves
modules.moduleDescription: Establishes the conceptual, ethical, and business case for accessibility before touching any implementation detail. This module builds the vocabulary and mental models you need to speak credibly with nonprofit clients, boards, and funders — people who need the 'why' before the 'how'.

resources.label: Web Accessibility Initiative – Accessibility Fundamentals
resources.description: A comprehensive overview from W3C WAI providing starting resources for learning web accessibility, including real-world user perspectives, essential components, accessibility principles, research findings, and free online courses.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: W3C WAI is the authoritative source your nonprofit clients will cite in RFPs and grant language, so grounding your practice here gives you the shared vocabulary that opens doors.
keyTopics: How people with disabilities use the web

resources.label: A11y 101
resources.description: An introductory web accessibility guide for both designers and developers covering foundational knowledge on assistive technology, design principles, inclusive design, and the legal and business case for accessibility.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: As someone onboarding nonprofit clients with mixed technical literacy, this is the resource you can hand off directly — it covers the business and legal case in plain language without requiring a development background.
keyTopics: Legal and business case for accessibility

resources.label: WebAIM – Web Accessibility In Mind
resources.description: A university-based nonprofit providing comprehensive web accessibility expertise through training, consulting, evaluation services, and free tools, serving organizations seeking to create accessible digital content.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: WebAIM publishes the annual accessibility survey data (WebAIM Million) that carries real social currency in the a11y community — citing it positions you as current rather than theoretical.

resources.label: W3C – World Wide Web Consortium
resources.description: The international standards body that develops specifications and guidelines for an accessible, internationalized, private, and secure web, bringing together member organizations, staff, and the public.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Understanding W3C's role as the standards body gives you the organizational map to navigate where WCAG, ARIA, and future specs come from — essential when advising clients on long-term compliance strategies.

resources.label: Law Office of Lainey Feingold – Digital Accessibility Ethics
resources.description: Lainey Feingold's professional website covering disability rights law, digital accessibility compliance, and Structured Negotiation — a collaborative dispute resolution approach that avoids litigation.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Nonprofit clients operating under ADA Title II regulations need a consultant who understands Structured Negotiation as a low-conflict compliance path — Feingold's work is the primary reference for that approach.

quizQuestions.question: A nonprofit board member pushes back on your accessibility proposal, saying their organization serves mostly elderly donors who 'aren't disabled.' How do you most effectively reframe accessibility's value to this audience?
options: Cite ADA Title II regulations and the legal penalties for noncompliance to shift the conversation to risk management
Explain that disability is a spectrum that includes age-related impairments, and that accessible design improves the experience for all users including their core donor base
Show WCAG compliance levels and explain that AA is the industry standard their peers are meeting
Argue that inclusive design principles require equal access regardless of who the primary audience is
quizQuestions.explanation: Connecting accessibility to the board's existing constituency — elderly donors who experience situational and age-related impairments — uses inclusive design framing to make the business and ethical case land simultaneously. This is more persuasive than leading with legal risk for an audience that doesn't yet see themselves as having accessibility stakeholders.

quizQuestions.question: An NGO client is responding to a foundation RFP that asks how their digital platforms support equitable access. You're drafting the accessibility section. Which approach best demonstrates consulting credibility and substance?
options: Reference WCAG AA conformance, name specific assistive technologies the site has been tested with, and cite a structured negotiation or legal precedent that establishes the standard of care
Describe the organization's commitment to inclusive design and mention that alt text and color contrast have been reviewed
List the accessibility features of their CMS platform and note that automated scanning tools show zero critical errors
Explain that the site meets Section 508 standards, which exceed what most nonprofits achieve
quizQuestions.explanation: A credible RFP response names the standard (WCAG AA), demonstrates real testing practice (specific assistive technologies), and anchors the claim in legal or advocacy precedent — the vocabulary and evidence signals that distinguish a practitioner from someone reading from a checklist.

quizQuestions.question: A nonprofit executive director asks why accessible procurement matters when they're just purchasing an off-the-shelf donor management platform. What is the strongest consulting response?
options: Accessible procurement ensures the vendor's product meets WCAG 2.1 AA so your staff and constituents with disabilities can use the platform without workarounds or exclusion
Procurement decisions are primarily a legal concern and should be reviewed by counsel before signing any software contract
Most SaaS platforms self-certify accessibility, so reviewing their documentation is sufficient due diligence
Accessible procurement is mainly relevant for government contracts and may not apply to nonprofit software purchases
quizQuestions.explanation: Accessible procurement frames the purchasing decision as a direct equity and usability issue for both staff and constituents — grounding the 'why' in the organization's own mission rather than abstract compliance, which is the persuasive register that moves nonprofit decision-makers.

modules.moduleTitle: WCAG in depth: understanding the standard you'll live by
modules.moduleDescription: Provides a layered, progressive reading of WCAG — from plain-language summary to full technical specification — so you can fluently discuss compliance levels with clients, interpret audit findings, and write defensible recommendations. Given your design systems background, the four-principle structure will map naturally onto component-level thinking.

resources.label: WCAG 2 at a Glance
resources.description: A paraphrased plain-language summary of WCAG 2.1 and 2.2 organized around four principles: Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, and Robust.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: This is the one-page version you reference when explaining compliance requirements to a nonprofit's board or grant writer — read it first to anchor the full spec in plain terms.

resources.label: WCAG 2 Overview – W3C WAI
resources.description: The authoritative overview of Web Content Accessibility Guidelines covering WCAG 2.0, 2.1, and 2.2 with success criteria at three conformance levels and supporting technical materials.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: You need command of the specific version differences (2.1 vs 2.2) because clients under recent ADA Title II rulemaking are being held to 2.1 AA as a legal baseline — knowing the delta matters for gap analysis.
keyTopics: Four principles: perceivable, operable, understandable, robust
Success criteria at three levels: A, AA, AAA

resources.label: A11y Project Checklist
resources.description: A comprehensive, task-based accessibility checklist grounded in WCAG 2.2, covering 18 categories of web accessibility concerns with specific, testable success criteria.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: This checklist translates WCAG's abstract success criteria into concrete, auditable line items — exactly the format you need when scoping a consulting engagement or training a nonprofit's internal team.

quizQuestions.question: During a WCAG audit debrief, your client asks why you flagged a missing skip navigation link as Level A rather than AA. They want to deprioritize it because 'it's just for keyboard users.' How do you explain the conformance level decision and push back on the prioritization logic?
options: Explain that Level A failures represent the minimum threshold below which a page is effectively unusable for some users, and that keyboard navigation is the baseline for screen reader, switch access, and voice control users — not a niche case
Agree that Level A issues can sometimes be lower priority if the affected user group is small, and suggest scheduling it for a future sprint
Note that skip navigation is a Level AA requirement, so it should be addressed alongside other AA items in the remediation backlog
Clarify that Level A applies only to static content pages and may not be required for interactive application views
quizQuestions.explanation: Level A criteria define the floor of accessibility — failure means the content is inaccessible to entire user groups, not just inconvenient. Correctly identifying keyboard navigation as the foundation for multiple assistive technologies counters the 'niche user' dismissal with accurate scope.

quizQuestions.question: A developer on a nonprofit client's team argues that since they're targeting WCAG 2.1 AA, they don't need to worry about 2.2 additions like Focus Appearance or Dragging Movements. How do you advise the team?
options: Confirm that 2.1 AA is the current legal baseline in most jurisdictions, but recommend proactively adopting 2.2 criteria that address real user needs — particularly Focus Appearance, which resolves common keyboard visibility failures already in their backlog
Agree that 2.2 compliance is optional and should only be pursued if a client specifically requests it in their contract scope
Explain that 2.2 supersedes 2.1 entirely and the team must now meet all new criteria or risk legal exposure
Suggest waiting until 2.2 becomes the formal ISO standard before incorporating any new success criteria into the workflow
quizQuestions.explanation: WCAG 2.2 is backwards-compatible with 2.1, and several new criteria address problems already visible in most audits — advising selective adoption based on real user impact is the practitioner move that builds credibility while managing client scope realistically.

quizQuestions.question: A nonprofit client's design system uses custom-styled div-based buttons throughout their application. Their developer says the components 'look and behave correctly.' From a WCAG Robust principle perspective, what is the core risk and what remediation guidance do you give?
options: Div elements lack the native semantics that browsers expose to the accessibility tree, meaning assistive technologies may not identify them as interactive controls — remediate by replacing with native button elements or adding precise ARIA role, name, and state attributes with full keyboard support
The Robust principle primarily concerns markup validation, so running an HTML validator and fixing reported errors will resolve any assistive technology compatibility issues
Custom div buttons are acceptable under WCAG if they pass automated contrast and focus indicator checks, since visual behavior is what the Robust principle evaluates
The risk is limited to older screen readers; modern NVDA and VoiceOver versions handle div-based interactive elements reliably without additional ARIA
quizQuestions.explanation: The Robust principle requires that content be interpretable by a wide range of assistive technologies, which depends on correct semantics in the accessibility tree — not visual appearance. Native HTML is the first-choice remediation because it carries implicit roles and keyboard behavior that ARIA must otherwise replicate entirely.

modules.moduleTitle: Semantic HTML and ARIA: building accessible structure
modules.moduleDescription: Covers the technical substrate of accessible web experiences — semantic HTML, ARIA roles and attributes, and how browsers expose these to assistive technology. For a consultant advising development teams at resource-constrained nonprofits, understanding the difference between 'use semantic HTML first' and 'add ARIA when necessary' is a force-multiplier for accurate, efficient remediation guidance.

resources.label: HTML: HyperText Markup Language – MDN
resources.description: MDN's comprehensive HTML reference and tutorial hub covering elements, attributes, semantic markup, forms, media elements, and web standards best practices.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: MDN is the daily reference tool of the developers you'll be advising — knowing its structure lets you point directly to authoritative documentation rather than paraphrasing in your deliverables.

resources.label: HTML5 Accessibility – Browser support state
resources.description: A reference tool that tests and reports on HTML5 feature accessibility support across major browsers, evaluating keyboard accessibility, platform API mapping, and whether ARIA workarounds are needed.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: When a nonprofit dev team asks whether a native HTML element is 'safe to use,' this gives you cross-browser evidence rather than opinion — critical for defensible technical recommendations.
resources.freshnessNote: Editor's draft last updated August 2020; browser support landscape has shifted significantly since then — verify against a11ysupport.io for current data.

resources.label: ARIA Authoring Practices Guide (APG)
resources.description: The W3C's comprehensive guide teaching developers how to use ARIA roles, states, and properties to create accessible web components, with design patterns, functional examples, and keyboard support guidance.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: The APG's design patterns are the canonical reference for evaluating whether a custom widget is correctly implemented — knowing it cold lets you audit interactive components with authority.

resources.label: Accessibility Support – AT compatibility database
resources.description: A community-driven database testing how ARIA roles, attributes, and HTML elements perform across real screen readers, voice control software, and browser combinations.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: The gap between 'technically correct ARIA' and 'actually works in JAWS with Chrome' is where real user failures live — this database is your evidence base for that gap when advising dev teams.
keyTopics: Voice control and speech recognition support

resources.label: WAI-ARIA screen reader compatibility – PowerMapper
resources.description: Comprehensive compatibility testing data for WAI-ARIA attributes and roles across major screen readers and browsers, with reliability percentages and historical trends since 2014.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: The longitudinal trend data from 2014–2025 lets you show clients that AT support has meaningfully improved — useful for countering 'we'll add accessibility later when it's more stable' objections.
keyTopics: Expected failures and correct usage patterns

resources.label: W3C Markup Validation Service
resources.description: A free tool that checks markup validity of web documents in HTML, XHTML, SMIL, and other formats via URI, file upload, or direct input.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Valid markup is the lowest-cost accessibility win — this tool lets you quickly demonstrate baseline HTML hygiene issues in a client's existing site during a discovery engagement.

quizQuestions.question: A nonprofit's development team is remediating a complex data table built entirely with div and span elements. The team lead proposes wrapping everything in a role='grid' with aria-labels instead of refactoring to native HTML table elements, citing timeline pressure. How do you advise them?
options: Recommend refactoring to semantic HTML table elements as the more reliable path — native table, thead, th with scope, and caption provide robust accessibility tree exposure with less implementation risk than a fully ARIA-constructed grid, which requires precise role and property management to avoid introducing new failures
Approve the ARIA grid approach since role='grid' is a well-supported ARIA pattern that major screen readers handle consistently, and it avoids a larger refactor
Suggest a hybrid: keep the div structure but add role='table' and aria-rowcount attributes, which will satisfy most screen reader compatibility requirements
Advise the team to add a visually hidden HTML table that mirrors the data, keeping the div structure intact for visual rendering
quizQuestions.explanation: The first rule of ARIA is to use native HTML when it exists — semantic table elements provide the correct accessibility tree relationships with far lower implementation risk than reconstructing equivalent semantics with ARIA, especially under timeline pressure at a resource-constrained nonprofit.

quizQuestions.question: You're reviewing a nonprofit's React application and notice that all navigation, main content, and footer regions are wrapped in generic div elements with CSS classes like .nav and .footer. A screen reader user on your testing team reports they cannot jump between sections efficiently. What is the root cause and your remediation guidance?
options: The application lacks ARIA landmark regions — replacing structural divs with nav, main, header, and footer HTML5 elements, or adding role='navigation', role='main' etc., will expose landmarks to screen readers and enable efficient region navigation via assistive technology shortcuts
The root cause is missing skip links — adding a 'skip to main content' anchor at the top of each page will resolve the navigation inefficiency without requiring structural changes
CSS class names are not exposed to the accessibility tree, so the fix is to add aria-label attributes to each div to give screen readers descriptive text for each section
The issue is likely a screen reader configuration problem, since modern browsers map div elements with semantic class names to the accessibility tree in most cases
quizQuestions.explanation: Landmark regions — exposed through HTML5 sectioning elements or explicit ARIA landmark roles — are the mechanism screen reader users rely on for page-level navigation; CSS classes have no accessibility tree presence, making semantic structure the correct remediation target.

quizQuestions.question: During a code review for a nonprofit client, you find a custom autocomplete search widget that uses aria-live='assertive' to announce every keystroke result update. Beta testers with screen readers report the experience as disruptive and unusable. What is the ARIA misuse and how do you correct it?
options: aria-live='assertive' interrupts the screen reader immediately for every update, which is appropriate only for critical alerts — replace with aria-live='polite' so result announcements queue after the user's current reading context, and consider debouncing announcements to fire only when results stabilize

The problem is that aria-live regions should not be used on search widgets at all — implement the ARIA combobox pattern with aria-expanded and aria-activedescendant instead, which handles result announcement natively
Reduce the frequency of DOM updates driving the live region so the assertive announcements occur less often, preserving the immediate interrupt behavior for accessibility
Switch to role='status' on the results container, which is equivalent to aria-live='assertive' but better supported across NVDA, JAWS, and VoiceOver
quizQuestions.explanation: aria-live='assertive' is designed for urgent, time-sensitive alerts — applying it to incremental search results creates constant interruptions that make the widget unusable; aria-live='polite' respects the user's current reading flow and is the correct live region value for non-critical dynamic content updates.

modules.moduleTitle: Auditing and testing: tools for evaluation and evidence
modules.moduleDescription: Equips you with the practical toolchain for conducting accessibility audits — automated scanning, contrast checking, and manual inspection — and frames each tool's appropriate scope and limitations. For a consulting practice, the ability to produce credible, reproducible audit evidence is a core service differentiator.

resources.label: WAVE Web Accessibility Evaluation Tools
resources.description: A comprehensive suite of web accessibility evaluation tools including browser extensions, subscription APIs, and stand-alone testing engines for evaluating WCAG compliance and identifying accessibility issues.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: WAVE's visual overlay approach makes accessibility issues immediately legible to non-technical nonprofit stakeholders — use it in client presentations to make the case for remediation investment.

resources.label: ANDI – Accessibility Testing Tool (SSA)
resources.description: A free browser bookmarklet from the Social Security Administration that automates accessibility testing, reveals screen reader output for interactive elements, and checks Section 508 compliance.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Federal nonprofits and NGOs receiving government funding often fall under Section 508 obligations — ANDI is the SSA's own tool, giving your recommendations federal-agency credibility in those contexts.

resources.label: WebAIM Color Contrast Checker
resources.description: An interactive tool calculating contrast ratios between foreground and background colors with immediate WCAG 2.0/2.1 AA and AAA pass/fail results for normal text, large text, and UI components.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Color contrast is typically the highest-frequency finding in any audit and the easiest win to explain to a design team — this is your daily-use tool for that conversation.
keyTopics: WCAG 2.0 and 2.1 compliance levels (AA and AAA)
Graphical objects and UI component contrast

resources.label: Colour Contrast Checker – ColorContrast.cc
resources.description: An interactive web tool evaluating color contrast ratios against WCAG standards with hex, RGB, and HSL input, real-time preview, and AA/AAA pass/fail results.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: When working with a nonprofit's brand team, the RGB/HSL slider interface here is more approachable than hex-only tools — useful for live color exploration during design reviews.
keyTopics: Hex, RGB, and HSL color input

resources.label: Live WebVTT Validator
resources.description: An interactive browser-based validator for WebVTT files used in video captions, descriptions, and metadata, providing real-time parsing and error detection with line-level feedback.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Nonprofits with video-heavy outreach content (advocacy, training, donor communications) frequently have malformed caption files — this quick validator surfaces those issues before they become audit findings.

quizQuestions.question: You deliver an automated audit report to a nonprofit client showing 14 accessibility violations, and their communications director responds: 'Great, we fixed all 14 — are we now accessible?' How do you set accurate expectations about audit completeness?
options: Explain that automated tools reliably detect only 20–30% of WCAG issues — issues like keyboard navigation order, screen reader announcement quality, cognitive load, and focus management require manual testing and assistive technology evaluation to surface
Confirm that resolving all automated findings brings the site to WCAG AA conformance, since automated tools are calibrated to the full AA success criteria set
Recommend running a second automated scan with a different tool to cross-validate findings before concluding the remediation phase is complete
Explain that automated audits cover all perceivable and operable criteria but that understandable and robust criteria require manual review to fully evaluate
quizQuestions.explanation: Automated tools are powerful for detecting specific, deterministic issues like missing alt attributes or contrast ratios, but the majority of WCAG failures — particularly those involving interaction, semantics, and assistive technology behavior — require human evaluation; overstating automated coverage is a credibility risk for any consulting practice.

quizQuestions.question: A nonprofit client asks you to evaluate two competing color palette options for their redesigned donation page. Palette A passes WCAG 2.1 AA contrast for normal text but fails for UI component boundaries. Palette B passes AA for all text and components but fails AAA for normal text. Which palette do you recommend and on what basis?
options: Recommend Palette B — WCAG 2.1 AA compliance requires 3:1 contrast for UI components and graphical objects in addition to text, making Palette A non-conformant at the required level regardless of its text performance, while Palette B meets all AA thresholds
Recommend Palette A because text contrast is the primary accessibility concern for donation forms, and UI component contrast is an enhanced criterion that applies only at AAA
Recommend Palette B only if the client has explicitly committed to AAA conformance in their accessibility statement, otherwise both palettes have tradeoffs that must be disclosed
Request a third option that achieves AAA for both text and components, since a donation page is a critical user journey that warrants the highest conformance level
quizQuestions.explanation: WCAG 2.1 Success Criterion 1.4.11 (Non-text Contrast) is a Level AA requirement — UI component boundaries must meet 3:1 contrast, making Palette A non-conformant at the standard compliance level consultants are typically engaged to achieve.

quizQuestions.question: You're scoping an accessibility audit for a nonprofit that hosts 200+ hours of archived program video with no captions. The client wants to know which tool in your audit workflow validates their existing WebVTT caption files for correctness before they invest in retroactive captioning. How do you frame tool selection and its limits in your audit plan?
options: Use a WebVTT validator to detect syntax errors and timing issues in existing files, but note that technical validity does not guarantee caption quality — accuracy, speaker identification, and sound description require human review against the audio, and live event content needs a separate captioning workflow assessment
Recommend running automated accessibility scan tools across the video pages, which will flag missing track elements and invalid caption file references as WCAG 1.2 failures
Explain that WebVTT validation is outside standard WCAG audit scope and should be handled by the video production team rather than included in the accessibility audit deliverable
Use the browser developer tools media panel to preview caption rendering, which provides sufficient quality assurance for a first-pass audit without specialized validation tooling
quizQuestions.explanation: A WebVTT validator addresses file-level syntax correctness, but caption accessibility under WCAG 1.2 requires human evaluation of accuracy, completeness, and meaningful description — distinguishing tool scope from quality judgment is exactly the kind of audit framing that builds consulting credibility.

modules.moduleTitle: Media accessibility: captions, alt text, and audio description
modules.moduleDescription: Focuses on accessible media — images, video, and audio description — through both technical practice and creative reframing. Nonprofit and NGO communications are often image- and video-rich, making this module directly applicable to real client deliverables and a strong differentiator for a consultant who can speak to both compliance and craft.

resources.label: Alt Text as Poetry
resources.description: A collaborative project by disabled artists reframing alt text as creative practice, offering a free workbook with writing exercises and workshops exploring thoughtful image description for blind and low-vision users.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Moving nonprofit communications teams from perfunctory alt text to genuinely descriptive writing requires a reframe, not just a rule — this resource provides the creative on-ramp that compliance checklists cannot.
keyTopics: Alt text as accessibility and poetry

resources.label: The Complete Guide to Captioning Videos – Meryl Evans
resources.description: A comprehensive guide to video captioning by a deaf accessibility expert, covering why captions matter, 10 best practices, caption statistics, and an extensive directory of captioning tools and services.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: The statistic that 86% of caption users are not deaf or hard of hearing is exactly the kind of data-backed business case argument that moves nonprofit leadership from 'nice to have' to 'must have'.
keyTopics: Why captions matter: reach, SEO, brand awareness, legal liability

quizQuestions.question: A nonprofit's communications team asks you to review their image alt text practice. Their current approach is to describe every image literally and in full detail. You notice a decorative divider graphic has the alt text 'Blue wavy line used as a section divider between paragraphs.' What guidance do you give and why does it matter beyond compliance?
options: Decorative images should have empty alt attributes (alt='') so screen readers skip them entirely — verbose alt text on decorative elements degrades the reading experience by injecting meaningless noise, and word economy in descriptions is as important as accuracy for users relying on audio rendering
The alt text is acceptable since it accurately describes the image; best practice is to err toward description rather than omission to avoid potential WCAG 1.1.1 violations
Recommend shortening to 'Decorative divider' to reduce verbosity while maintaining the accessible name for users who may encounter it via screen reader
Suggest moving the decorative image to CSS as a background image, which removes the need for any alt text decision and is always the preferred pattern
quizQuestions.explanation: WCAG 1.1.1 explicitly permits empty alt for decorative images, and the craft dimension of alt text — drawing on the module's framing of alt text as writing practice — requires knowing when silence is the right choice; unnecessary descriptions burden screen reader users with content that adds no meaning.

quizQuestions.question: A nonprofit client is launching a fundraising campaign video with no audio description. Their video producer says the narrator already describes most of the visuals in the voiceover. As a consultant, how do you evaluate whether audio description is actually required?
options: Assess whether all meaningful visual information — text overlays, on-screen actions, facial expressions conveying emotion, and graphic elements — is conveyed by the existing audio; if any visual content necessary to understand the video is absent from the narration, audio description is required under WCAG 1.2.5 (AA) for prerecorded video

Audio description is only required under WCAG 1.2.3, which is Level A — since the client is targeting AA conformance, audio description for prerecorded content is optional if the narrator covers key visuals
If the video has synchronized captions, audio description is not required because captions provide an equivalent text alternative for users who cannot perceive the video
Recommend submitting the video to an automated audio description tool to scan the narration transcript against the visual timeline and flag gaps requiring description
quizQuestions.explanation: WCAG 1.2.5 requires audio description for all prerecorded video where the existing soundtrack doesn't fully convey visual content — the test is completeness of information transfer, not whether a narrator is present, and a consultant who can apply that test credibly distinguishes themselves from checklist-only practitioners.

quizQuestions.question: During a strategy session, a nonprofit's marketing director asks why they should invest in closed captions for their social media videos when auto-captions are already generated by the platform. How do you make the case using both accessibility and business rationale?
options: Auto-captions have significant accuracy errors — particularly for proper nouns, technical terms, and accented speakers common in nonprofit communications — which creates legal liability, erodes brand credibility, and fails users who rely on captions for comprehension; accurate closed captions also improve SEO indexing and expand reach to the 85% of social video watched without sound

Auto-captions are sufficient for WCAG compliance since the standard requires captions to be present, not to meet a specific accuracy threshold, so the investment case is primarily a brand preference decision
Closed captions are only legally required for video hosted on the organization's own website; social media platforms assume captioning responsibility for content published through their interfaces
Recommend open captions burned into the video as the most accessible option, since they eliminate any dependency on platform caption rendering behavior
quizQuestions.explanation: The module frames captions as a convergence of accessibility compliance, legal risk, SEO value, and audience reach — making the case on all four dimensions simultaneously is the consulting move that shifts captions from a disability accommodation to a communications strategy asset.

modules.moduleDescription: Addresses accessibility on native mobile platforms — React Native and Flutter — alongside mobile-specific WCAG considerations. As nonprofits increasingly deliver services and communications through mobile-first experiences, consultants who can evaluate and guide mobile accessibility work have a meaningful competitive advantage.

resources.label: React Native Accessibility Documentation
resources.description: Comprehensive reference documentation for implementing accessibility in React Native apps across Android and iOS, covering 25+ accessibility properties and APIs for screen reader support and assistive technology integration.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: React Native is the dominant cross-platform mobile stack for resource-constrained nonprofits — knowing its accessibility API surface lets you audit or spec mobile apps without a separate native specialist.

resources.label: Flutter Accessibility Documentation
resources.description: Flutter's official accessibility documentation covering core principles, regulations, framework support, assistive technologies, UI design patterns, testing, and an accessibility release checklist.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Flutter adoption is growing among NGOs building for global, multilingual audiences — this documentation lets you evaluate Flutter projects with the same rigor you apply to web work.
keyTopics: Assistive technologies and screen reader support

quizQuestions.question: You're reviewing a React Native mobile app built for a nonprofit's client intake process. The development team has added accessibilityLabel to every touchable element, but users testing with iOS VoiceOver report that form field purpose is still unclear. What is the most likely gap in their implementation?
options: accessibilityLabel alone provides an accessible name but does not communicate the element's role or its current state — the team likely needs to also implement accessibilityRole to identify inputs as form fields and accessibilityHint to convey expected input, so VoiceOver users understand both what the control is and what action it performs
The issue is that iOS VoiceOver requires aria-label rather than accessibilityLabel in React Native — the team should migrate to aria-* properties for cross-platform compatibility
accessibilityLabel values are being overridden by the underlying native view's default label; the team should set accessible={true} explicitly on each element to ensure custom labels are applied
VoiceOver clarity issues in forms are typically caused by missing focus management — the team should implement accessibilityViewIsModal to constrain VoiceOver navigation within each form section
quizQuestions.explanation: An accessible name (accessibilityLabel) is only one of three properties VoiceOver uses to describe a control — role and state complete the announcement, and without accessibilityRole, users hear a label with no information about whether it is a button, text field, or toggle.

quizQuestions.question: A nonprofit client's Flutter development team is preparing to release a new service-finder feature. They ask you what accessibility criteria should be on their pre-release checklist specifically for Android TalkBack users. What do you prioritize?
options: Verify that all interactive elements have meaningful semantic labels, that accessibilityLiveRegion is configured correctly for dynamic content updates so TalkBack announces changes without requiring user-initiated focus, and that custom gestures have accessible alternatives since TalkBack's gesture set can conflict with in-app swipe interactions
Confirm that the Flutter app passes automated accessibility scanning in Android Studio, which validates TalkBack compatibility for all standard widget types without requiring device testing
Focus on color contrast and text scaling since TalkBack users have the highest rate of low vision co-occurrence, making visual accessibility the most impactful checklist category for this audience
Ensure the app requests the BIND_ACCESSIBILITY_SERVICE permission correctly so TalkBack can interface with the application's view hierarchy during navigation
quizQuestions.explanation: TalkBack-specific failures cluster around unlabeled interactive elements, live region misconfiguration that causes missed or duplicate announcements, and gesture conflicts — a release checklist anchored to these categories catches the failures that automated tools miss and that most significantly degrade the user experience.

quizQuestions.question: A nonprofit organization wants to understand whether their mobile-first web experience meets accessibility standards for users on iOS using VoiceOver. Their team has only tested on desktop with NVDA. How do you explain the gap in their current testing approach and what do you recommend?
options: Desktop screen reader testing with NVDA does not replicate mobile VoiceOver behavior — iOS VoiceOver uses touch-based navigation gestures, announces content in a different order based on DOM source rather than visual layout, and has distinct support characteristics for ARIA patterns; the team needs device-based testing with VoiceOver on iOS to validate the mobile experience
NVDA desktop testing is a reliable proxy for VoiceOver mobile behavior since both screen readers consume the same accessibility tree exposed by the browser's rendering engine
Recommend using browser developer tools' mobile emulation mode with NVDA, which simulates the touch interaction model and provides equivalent coverage to physical device testing
The gap is primarily in gesture support — supplement NVDA testing with a touch event simulation library to cover swipe navigation patterns that VoiceOver uses on iOS
quizQuestions.explanation: iOS VoiceOver and NVDA differ in interaction model, announcement behavior, and ARIA support characteristics — mobile accessibility testing requires actual device testing with the target platform's screen reader, and advising clients to close this gap is a direct deliverable differentiator for a consulting practice.

modules.moduleDescription: Connects you to the active practitioner community where accessibility norms, best practices, and professional reputation are shaped. For someone building a consulting practice, being fluent in current debates and knowing the recognized experts is as important as technical knowledge — this is how you develop the social currency the learner profile explicitly values.

resources.label: Smashing Magazine – Accessibility Articles
resources.description: A curated collection of 161 articles on web accessibility covering inclusive design, assistive technologies, WCAG compliance, accessible UX research, and design tools from practitioners across the field.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Smashing Magazine bridges design and development in exactly the way your UX background positions you — it's where the practical pattern-level thinking lives that you can translate directly into client recommendations.
keyTopics: Design for deaf and hard of hearing users

resources.label: Adrian Roselli – Blog
resources.description: Adrian Roselli's blog featuring in-depth, often critical articles on ARIA implementation, WCAG compliance, screen reader support, accessibility overlays, and practical accessibility barriers.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Roselli is one of the most-cited practitioners in the a11y community and his posts regularly debunk vendor claims — reading him keeps your consulting advice from being captured by overlay tool marketing or other industry hype.

resources.label: TetraLogical
resources.description: An accessibility consultancy specializing in inclusive design, offering assessments, strategy, development support, and team training across web and app accessibility.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: TetraLogical publishes practitioner-grade technical articles and their team (including Bruce Lawson and Léonie Watson) is active in standards work — following their output positions you in the same professional tier.

quizQuestions.question: A nonprofit client's vendor is pitching an accessibility overlay product, claiming it will bring the organization's website into immediate WCAG 2.1 AA compliance and eliminate legal risk. The client asks for your professional assessment. What do you tell them?
options: Overlay products are widely criticized by the accessibility practitioner community — they do not fix underlying code failures, are frequently bypassed or broken when used with real assistive technologies, and have been named in ADA litigation; recommend allocating that budget to structural remediation and genuine user testing instead
Overlays can be a reasonable interim solution while a full remediation project is scoped, provided the vendor supplies an updated VPAT showing conformance claims
The legal protection claim is accurate since overlays automatically update to track WCAG standard revisions, which removes ongoing compliance maintenance burden from the organization
Evaluate the specific overlay product against current screen reader compatibility data before advising the client, since overlay quality varies significantly across vendors
quizQuestions.explanation: The practitioner community consensus — documented in open letters, legal records, and screen reader compatibility testing — is that overlays fail real users and do not confer legal protection; a consultant who can cite this credibly and redirect budget toward effective remediation is providing genuine value rather than validating a harmful vendor narrative.

quizQuestions.question: During a community of practice discussion, a colleague argues that CAPTCHA is acceptable on nonprofit donation forms because accessibility is addressed by offering an audio alternative. How do you respond based on current practitioner understanding?
options: Audio CAPTCHAs are notoriously unusable for users with cognitive disabilities and those using screen readers, and CAPTCHA as a pattern has well-documented accessibility failures that audio alternatives do not resolve — current best practice favors invisible bot detection methods like honeypot fields or behavioral analysis that eliminate the CAPTCHA barrier entirely
Audio CAPTCHA satisfies WCAG 1.1.1 by providing an alternative for users who cannot perceive the visual challenge, making it a compliant and professionally defensible accessibility accommodation
CAPTCHA accessibility depends on implementation — advise the client to select a CAPTCHA vendor with a current VPAT showing AA conformance before making a recommendation
The real issue is that nonprofit donation forms are exempt from CAPTCHA requirements if they use a payment processor that handles its own fraud detection
quizQuestions.explanation: CAPTCHA is a known accessibility failure point across disability categories — cognitive, visual, and motor — and the practitioner community has moved well past audio alternatives as a satisfactory solution; recommending elimination of the pattern rather than accommodation of it signals current, community-informed expertise.

quizQuestions.question: You are writing an Accessibility Conformance Report (VPAT) for a nonprofit's web application and need to document partial support for a WCAG success criterion. A colleague suggests marking it 'Supports' since the feature works for most users. What is the professional and community norm you apply?
options: Mark it 'Partially Supports' and document the specific conditions under which it fails — VPATs are legal and procurement documents where overstating conformance creates liability and damages trust with institutional clients who conduct their own testing; accurate partial support documentation is a credibility signal, not a weakness

Use 'Supports' with a remarks note explaining the edge cases, since 'Partially Supports' may cause the organization to lose procurement opportunities against competitors with cleaner conformance reports
Escalate to legal counsel before marking any criterion as partially supported, since VPAT language has direct implications for ADA compliance representations
Mark 'Not Evaluated' to avoid making an inaccurate claim, which is preferable to partial support documentation when the testing evidence is incomplete
quizQuestions.explanation: VPAT accuracy is a professional integrity issue with direct legal and procurement consequences — the practitioner community norm is precise, honest documentation of partial support with specific failure conditions, which builds long-term client trust and withstands scrutiny from enterprise accessibility reviewers.

modules.moduleDescription: Maps out structured learning paths toward professional certification, particularly IAAP credentials that carry weight with enterprise and institutional clients. For a consultant building credibility with nonprofits and NGOs, CPACC or WAS certification provides an external signal of expertise that shortens the trust-building process with new clients.
modules.estimatedReadTime: Self-paced — 20–80 hours depending on certification track

resources.label: Web Workshop – Accessible Web Development (Dimensions Project)
resources.description: A structured open-source web development curriculum designed for students who are blind, covering HTML, CSS, and JavaScript with accessibility-first pedagogy including prerecorded lectures and tactile diagram support.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: The pedagogy of teaching web development to blind students directly embodies the 'design for the edges, benefit everyone' principle — studying how this curriculum is structured sharpens your own accessibility-first teaching approach for client workshops.
resources.freshnessNote: Most recent course iteration was August–October 2020; curriculum materials may still be valid but the live cohort model appears inactive — verify availability before referencing to clients.

resources.label: Deque University
resources.description: A comprehensive digital accessibility training platform offering self-paced online courses, instructor-led training, and reference materials covering web, document, and mobile accessibility, with IAAP certification preparation and scholarships for people with disabilities.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Deque University's CPACC and WAS preparation courses are the industry-standard certification pathway — completing or recommending them signals professional-grade commitment to accessibility that differentiates your practice in nonprofit RFP processes.

quizQuestions.question: A prospective nonprofit client asks why they should hire a CPACC-certified consultant rather than a UX designer who knows accessibility well. How do you articulate the credential's specific value in a consulting context?
options: CPACC certification demonstrates validated knowledge of disability theory, international accessibility standards, and the legal and technical landscape across domains — for a nonprofit client, it provides an external, credential-based signal of expertise that reduces due diligence burden and is recognized by enterprise and institutional funders who evaluate vendor qualifications
CPACC is primarily valuable for accessibility managers in-house and is less differentiating for consulting engagements where demonstrated audit deliverables and client references are more persuasive
The credential signals commitment to the field but has the same practical weight as WCAG familiarity, since no jurisdiction requires IAAP certification for accessibility consulting work

CPACC is most relevant for procurement contexts where federal contractors must demonstrate staff qualifications — for nonprofit clients, WAS certification is the more applicable credential since it validates technical implementation knowledge
quizQuestions.explanation: CPACC's value in a consulting context is precisely its function as an external credibility signal that shortens trust-building with new clients — particularly institutional funders and nonprofit boards who evaluate consultant qualifications but lack the domain expertise to assess accessibility knowledge directly.

quizQuestions.question: You're designing a half-day accessibility training workshop for a nonprofit's development team. The team lead requests that you focus on ARIA patterns and JavaScript since 'the team knows HTML.' Based on accessible pedagogy and the actual distribution of audit findings, how do you push back on this scope?
options: Redirect the scope to begin with semantic HTML foundations and landmark structure before ARIA — most real-world accessibility failures in development teams stem from incorrect use of div-based layouts and missing native semantics, not from ARIA implementation gaps, and ARIA patterns taught without that foundation tend to produce new violations rather than fixing existing ones

Accept the scope since the team's self-assessment is the best starting point for a half-day session, and ARIA and JavaScript patterns are the highest-complexity and highest-value skills for a development team to acquire
Propose splitting the session equally between HTML review and ARIA patterns, covering both to ensure no knowledge gaps remain after the training
Focus the workshop on screen reader testing with NVDA and VoiceOver so the team can empirically discover their own HTML and ARIA failures, which is more effective than instructor-led content delivery
quizQuestions.explanation: The first rule of ARIA — use native HTML when it exists — means that teams who reach for ARIA before mastering semantic HTML create more accessibility failures than they solve; accessible pedagogy for development teams requires correcting the foundation before layering complexity, regardless of the team's self-reported proficiency.

crossCuttingThemes: WCAG 2.x as the shared compliance language across web, mobile, and document contexts
The gap between automated testing and real-world assistive technology behavior
Business and legal case for accessibility as a consulting entry point
Semantic HTML as the foundation that reduces ARIA complexity
Color contrast as the highest-frequency, most explainable audit finding
Disability justice framing alongside technical compliance
Community-validated norms versus vendor claims (especially re: overlays)
Nonprofit/NGO context: resource constraints, video-heavy comms, ADA Title II exposure
notableGaps: PDF and document accessibility (WCAG for PDFs, remediation workflows) — critical for nonprofits producing grant reports, annual reports, and policy documents
Procurement and accessible vendor evaluation — how to write accessibility requirements into RFPs, evaluate ACRs/VPATs, and hold SaaS vendors accountable
User research with disabled participants — methodology for inclusive usability testing rather than audit-only evaluation
Accessibility program maturity models — how to assess and grow an organization's internal accessibility capability over time
Overlay and third-party tool evaluation — a dedicated resource critically analyzing automated overlay products that clients frequently ask about
Cognitive and learning disabilities — the resources here skew toward visual and motor impairments; COGA guidance is underrepresented
Social media and third-party platform accessibility — nonprofits with active social presence need guidance on accessible content beyond owned web properties
Cost estimation and ROI framing for accessibility work — practical tools for scoping and pricing consulting engagements for under-resourced clients
